payload.text:
Remove the view mode toggle component and the state logic controlling the switch between overview and list modes

Set the application to render the list view layout permanently by default

Create a new dashboard header section titled Production Achievement positioned immediately above the machine list container

Calculate and display the aggregate summary data previously shown in the overview mode including Total Plan and Total Finish and Achievement Percentage within this new header

Ensure the new header styling is consistent with the existing theme using standard tailwind css classes

Maintain the single file structure including the react and react dom and babel standalone cdn scripts to prevent the white screen issue

Keep the favicon link tag href attribute set to data colon comma to avoid 404 errors

**Eliminating View Toggle**

I'm removing the `viewMode` state and its associated toggle. My focus is now on streamlining the interface by permanently displaying the machine list. A new dashboard header will be integrated. I'm focusing on the code now.

I'm integrating a "Production Achievement" header above the machine list, replacing the old overview's data. I'll display "Total Plan", "Total Finish", and "Achievement Percentage". I've removed the individual "Plan", "Fin", and "Achv" labels above the machine grid. Style-wise, I'm staying consistent with existing Tailwind classes and light/dark theme support, maintaining the single `index.html` structure and `data:,` favicon.


**Refining Content Integration**

I'm removing the old code and implementing the "Production Achievement" header as the main focus. I'm focusing on the `index.html` structure. It's a more prominent replacement for the old overview. The prompt suggests a particular HTML file structure, and I'm updating it accordingly.

entries.description: Remove view mode logic, set list view as default, and add a new prominent Production Achievement header section replacing the old summary bar.

payload.text:
Add a refreshInterval state variable (defaulting to 120) and a timeRemaining state variable to track the countdown.

Implement a useEffect hook to handle the timer logic: decrement timeRemaining by 1 every second.

Trigger the data refresh function automatically when timeRemaining reaches 0, then reset timeRemaining back to the refreshInterval value.

Display the countdown timer (e.g., "Auto-refresh: 115s") in the top header alongside the summary metrics, keeping the font size small (text-xs or text-sm).

Add a "System Config" button or gear icon in the top header area.

Create a simple modal or input field that opens when "System Config" is clicked, allowing the user to type a new duration (in seconds).

Ensure that saving the new configuration updates the refreshInterval and immediately resets the timeRemaining counter to this new value.

Strictly maintain the single-file structure with React/Babel CDNs to prevent the white screen issue.

**Implementing Auto-Refresh Feature**

I'm currently focused on implementing an auto-refresh feature. I've defined the key state variables, including `refreshInterval` with a default of 120 seconds and the `timeRemaining` countdown. I'm building out the countdown logic, intending to decrement `timeRemaining` every second to drive the refresh cycle. The plan is to trigger a refresh action when the countdown reaches zero.


**Adding Refresh Cycle Logic**

I've just added the logic to trigger `fetchSheetData` and reset `timeRemaining` when the countdown reaches zero. Now, I'm working on displaying the countdown in the UI. I plan to add an "Auto-refresh: Xs" display to the top header. I am considering implementing a "System Config" button in the top header.


**Refining UI Display**

I'm now focused on the UI, specifically the top header. I'm updating the layout to accommodate the timer display alongside existing summary metrics. I'm thinking of either expanding the grid to five columns or adjusting the existing layout to fit. The countdown timer, displaying as "Auto-refresh: Xs," will be integrated into this header. I'm also considering either duplicating or moving the existing "System Config" button.


**Updating Core Implementation**

I'm now focusing on integrating the auto-refresh logic into the existing structure. I've updated the `App` states and the `useEffect` hook for the timer. I'm replacing the old `setInterval` with the new logic. Also, I'm working to get the refresh cycle to update the existing data fetch call. I'm also updating the header HTML and will update the Config Modal HTML to be consistent. I've revised the summary bar's grid to a five-column layout: Total Plan, Total Finish, Achv %, Cycle Eff, and Auto-refresh.

payload.text:
Locate the container element in the header that holds the page number and the previous and next navigation buttons

Add the Tailwind CSS class hidden to this container to ensure the pagination controls do not appear on mobile screens

Add the Tailwind CSS class md flex or md block to the same container so that the controls automatically become visible on desktop and tablet screens

Verify that the removal of the pagination on mobile does not disrupt the alignment of the production metrics or the countdown timer

Maintain the single file index html structure with React and Babel standalone CDN links to prevent white screen issues

Ensure the previous and next buttons still use the less than and greater than symbols when viewed on desktop mode

Keep the auto refresh timer and system config button visible and functional on both mobile and desktop views

**Considering Responsive Design**

I'm focusing on making the pagination controls responsive. My current plan is to hide the controls in the header on smaller screens. Right now, the pagination is part of the header grid, so I need to separate it for conditional display. I think this will be an effective solution, as the current layout complicates responsive design.

I've located the container holding the pagination controls within the header. I'm adding `hidden` to this container, which will hide the pagination on smaller screens. For desktop views, I plan to introduce `md:flex` (or possibly `md:block`) to restore its visibility. This approach offers a clean, straightforward way to handle responsive display.


**Adjusting Grid Layout**

I'm now adjusting the grid layout for mobile views. Specifically, I'm modifying the header grid to use `grid-cols-4` on smaller screens and `grid-cols-5` on medium-sized screens and up. I've also confirmed that the "Page" column container has the necessary classes to hide on mobile and re-appear on medium-sized screens. The right borders will be handled properly.

I'm now revising the border conditions for the Efficiency column. My goal is to hide the right border on mobile but show it on desktop. Since the "Page" column is hidden on mobile, `last:border-0` incorrectly targets it. I need to specifically target the Efficiency column, ensuring the border is conditionally applied based on screen size. I'll need to modify the grid classes to accommodate this.


**Refining Border Conditions**

I'm now refining the border conditions across the grid columns to manage visibility across different screen sizes. My focus is on ensuring the fourth column doesn't display a right border on mobile views, while still showing it on desktop. I've confirmed I need to adjust the class definitions for columns 1, 2, 3, and 4 to accurately reflect the desired mobile and desktop border behavior. I'm checking the current html provided to see the user's current progress.


**Refining Alignment**

I'm now focusing on column alignment within the header. Specifically, I want to keep the overall structure while revising the column's flexbox alignment. I'm modifying the header grid structure within `index.html` to align content more effectively across different screen sizes, ensuring the cycle efficiency stays visually consistent.


**Analyzing Border Visibility**

I'm now analyzing the border visibility for grid columns 1 through 4. My goal is to ensure the right border of the fourth column is hidden on mobile, but displayed on desktop. The class definition for the fourth column needs conditional application. On mobile, the fourth item `last:border-0` does not apply. I will need to refine my logic for columns 1-4 to accurately control the right border based on screen size. The flexbox alignment is the primary focus.


**Confirming Border Logic**

I will now apply the `hidden` and `md:flex` classes to the header pagination, which should fix the issue with the user's progress.

entries.description: Hide the pagination controls in the production summary header on mobile screens using Tailwind's responsive hidden/md:flex classes and adjust grid columns for alignment.

payload.text:
Add state variables named touchStart and touchEnd to the main application component to track the user finger position on the screen

Create a handleTouchStart function that records the clientX coordinate from the first touch point when the user touches the screen

Create a handleTouchMove function that updates the touchEnd state with the current coordinate as the finger drags across the screen

Implement a handleTouchEnd function to calculate the horizontal distance between the starting and ending touch points

Add logic inside handleTouchEnd to trigger the next page function if the swipe distance is negative and exceeds a minimum threshold indicating a left swipe

Add logic inside handleTouchEnd to trigger the previous page function if the swipe distance is positive and exceeds a minimum threshold indicating a right swipe

Attach the onTouchStart and onTouchMove and onTouchEnd event listeners to the main container div surrounding the machine list

Ensure the pagination button container retains the hidden class for mobile screens and the md flex class for desktop screens

Maintain the single file index html structure with React and Babel standalone CDN links to ensure the application runs correctly on GitHub Pages

**Considering Mobile Navigation**

I'm currently focused on implementing swipe-to-paginate functionality for mobile users on the machine list. I've identified the need for `touchStart` and `touchEnd` state variables. I'm also planning to implement `handleTouchStart` and `handleTouchMove` functions to interpret the swipe gestures.


**Refining Swipe Implementation**

I'm now refining the swipe logic for the mobile machine list pagination. I've fleshed out the `handleTouchEnd` function, incorporating distance calculations to determine swipe direction. I am setting the `touchStart` state with `e.targetTouches[0].clientX` on touch start, and setting `touchEnd` with `e.targetTouches[0].clientX` on touch move. Threshold logic using `minSwipeDistance` is under development to trigger page navigation, focusing on `isLeftSwipe` for the "next page" logic.


**Revising Swipe Logic**

I've realized a correction is needed for the swipe direction interpretation; it was reversed. Now, I'm adjusting the `isRightSwipe` and `isLeftSwipe` conditions in the `handleTouchEnd` function to reflect the standard behavior: Swipe Left (touchStart > touchEnd) for next page, and Swipe Right (touchStart < touchEnd) for previous page. Additionally, I am reevaluating the insertion of the state and handlers, and planning to wrap the container in the `index.html`.


**Implementing Swipe Functionality**

I've integrated the finalized swipe implementation into the machine list's container. I'm leveraging `onTouchStart`, `onTouchMove`, and `onTouchEnd` event handlers for touch interactions. The `onTouchStart` handler captures the initial touch position, while `onTouchMove` tracks the touch's movement. Finally, `onTouchEnd` determines swipe direction using distance calculations against `minSwipeDistance` to trigger page navigation. I am now evaluating implementation.

entries.description: Implemented swipe-to-paginate functionality for mobile users by adding touch state tracking (touchStart, touchEnd) and event handlers to the main content container. Left swipe navigates to the next page and right swipe navigates to the previous page.

payload.text:

Render a ResponsiveContainer with a fixed height such as 300 pixels to ensure the chart fits within the layout

Inside the container render a BarChart component with the transformed data linked to the data prop

Add an XAxis component with the dataKey set to the machine name or identifier to label the horizontal axis

Add a YAxis component to display the percentage scale on the vertical axis

Add a Tooltip component to show exact values when hovering over the bars

Add a Bar component with the dataKey set to the efficiency value and apply a fill color such as blue 600 to match the theme

Insert the CycleAchievementChart component into the main App component immediately below the summary header and above the machine list

Wrap the chart component in a conditional check to ensure it only renders when data is available to avoid errors

Maintain the single file structure with React and Babel standalone CDN links to prevent white screen issues

Preserve all existing features including swipe navigation the countdown timer and the system configuration modal

**Implementing Cycle Chart Component**

I'm currently focused on integrating the Recharts library into the application using the CDN link provided. The next step is crafting the `CycleAchievementChart` component and figuring out how to transform the machine data to align with Recharts' format, specifically mapping Machine Name and Cycle into the chart structure.


**Integrating Recharts into App**

I'm now diving into the specifics of implementing the CycleAchievementChart using Recharts. I'm focusing on leveraging `ResponsiveContainer`, `BarChart`, `XAxis`, `YAxis`, `Tooltip`, and `Bar` from the Recharts UMD bundle. My plan is to set the bar color to a blue theme and insert the chart strategically. I need to make sure the chart only renders if machine data is present and, most importantly, preserve existing app features. I'm checking the UMD script to confirm component availability under the `Recharts` global.


**Refining Chart Placement**

I've decided to refine the chart's integration, placing it directly below the summary header and above the machine list for optimal visibility. I've also incorporated the Recharts library via the provided CDN link. I'm now constructing the `CycleAchievementChart` component. I'm importing the necessary Recharts components, including `BarChart`, `Bar`, `XAxis`, `YAxis`, `CartesianGrid`, `Tooltip`, `Legend`, and `ResponsiveContainer`. I'm aiming for an aesthetically pleasing chart design.

entries.description: Added Recharts library, created CycleAchievementChart component, and integrated it into the main dashboard view to visualize machine efficiencies. Maintained swipe navigation, countdown timer, and configuration features.

payload.text:
Fix the following errors:

```
Uncaught ReferenceError: Recharts is not defined
```

**Analyzing Recharts Inclusion**

I'm currently investigating a `ReferenceError: Recharts is not defined`. I've examined the HTML, and Recharts is indeed included using the UMD build. Typically, with UMD builds, the library should expose itself globally, which seems to be the issue here. Further inspection may reveal conflicts or loading order inconsistencies. I'm focusing on why the global object isn't being created.


**Pinpointing Dependencies Issues**

I believe the core problem stems from asynchronous loading or a naming mismatch. Specifically, the `type="text/babel"` script is the likely culprit for the `ReferenceError: Recharts is not defined`. I'm now examining the dependencies; Recharts depends on `React` and `ReactDOM`, and I'm comparing the script inclusion to the React and ReactDOM script tags, ensuring their order is correct and the versions are compatible.


**Verifying Global Availability**

My concern now centers on *when* `Recharts` becomes globally accessible. Even with correct order, modern browsers may have loading conflicts. I'm checking if the script URL is valid and the variable name is `window.Recharts`. My previous focus on Prop-Types now seems less relevant, considering the error context. It looks like it is not a top level object.


**Investigating Modular Integration**

I'm now shifting gears after reviewing the file context. It appears the error stems from integrating Recharts into a modular application structure using Vite, rather than the standalone `index.html` setup. The provided `App.tsx` does *not* import Recharts directly. Instead, a custom component, `CycleAchievementChart`, references Recharts components, but it is not imported anywhere either. My next step will be to verify the correct import and usage within the modular context. I will also examine where and how `CycleAchievementChart` is imported.


**Reconciling Contextual Clues**

I've realized a significant discrepancy. The `App.tsx` file from the modular context, provided earlier, *doesn't* contain the `CycleAchievementChart` component. However, the *last* `index.html` file provided *does* include the Recharts components and the chart. Given this and the Vite context, the issue likely lies in the modular application structure, as it's missing Recharts code and dependency declarations in `package.json`. It seems I need to clarify which setup the user intends.


**Refining Script Integration**

I'm now zeroing in on the provided `index.html` file, recognizing it as the primary point of failure based on the original prompt instructions. It's imperative to ensure Recharts is correctly loaded as a global object. I need to verify that `window.Recharts` is accessible after script execution, which is the most reliable check for UMD compatibility. I'm focusing on strengthening the `index.html` file; however, the UMD file itself may contain a subtle bug, so I may have to edit the Recharts UMD script manually.


**Confirming CDN Reliability**

My immediate concern is the CDN reliability. I'll test and update the `index.html` to a more stable CDN link for Recharts, then ensure `window.Recharts` access. I will also add `prop-types` to explicitly avoid dependency issues with Recharts' UMD version. The modular structure may be misleading, so I'm focusing on strengthening the `index.html` file in alignment with the instructions, and will revisit modular application structure if that does not resolve the `ReferenceError`.


**Clarifying Application Integration**

I'm now going back to the core integration, where I'll update the `index.html` file to correctly reference `window.Recharts`, and also check the modular files. The prompt was "Add the Recharts library...Insert the CycleAchievementChart component...immediately below the summary header". My earlier focus on the modular setup was correct; I'm now verifying the instructions, because the user provided base versions of the files *before* the recent additions. I will clarify the expected integration with the user.


**Synchronizing Module Integration**

I'm now synchronizing the provided files. I will add the chart to `App.tsx` and correct the Recharts reference in `index.html`. It appears the error stems from integrating Recharts in the `index.html` setup, and not importing it using a modular approach. The UMD approach is likely failing because `React` and `ReactDOM` must be present on `window` prior to Recharts loading.


**Confirming Correct Loading**

I'm now revisiting the `index.html` file, which is likely the key integration point. I'll ensure React and ReactDOM are loaded **before** Recharts in the `<head>` section, ensuring the `window` environment is prepared. Subsequently, the script's contents must be reevaluated in that context. I will then ensure `window.Recharts` is accessible; that should resolve the `ReferenceError`.


**Verifying Precise CDN Versions**

I'm now focusing on a robust `index.html` fix. I will use a specific, reliable Recharts UMD version from a CDN, and ensure that `window.Recharts` is available. My previous modular plan is secondary; I'll still make sure modular works later. I will also add dependencies in a `package.json` file for good measure. I'll insert a `CycleAchievementChart` component. I see the user prefers the `index.html` method.

To fix the `ReferenceError: Recharts is not defined` and ensure the charting functionality works correctly in both the single-file and modular versions of your application, we need to ensure the library is correctly imported or accessed globally.

### Specification of Updates

1.  **`index.html`**:
    *   Updated the Recharts CDN link to a specific stable version (`2.12.7`) to ensure UMD compatibility.
    *   Added the `prop-types` dependency, which is often required by the Recharts UMD build to initialize correctly in the browser.
    *   Explicitly accessed `Recharts` via `window.Recharts` to prevent reference errors during the Babel compilation/execution phase.
2.  **`package.json`**:
    *   Added `recharts` and `prop-types` to the dependencies to support the modular development environment (Vite).
3.  **Modular Files (`App.tsx` & new component)**:
    *   Created `components/CycleAchievementChart.tsx` to modularize the chart logic.
    *   Updated `App.tsx` to import the chart and include the touch/swipe state logic and the chart component itself, synchronizing it with the features present in the `index.html`.

entries.description: Added recharts and prop-types dependencies for modular environment support.
Created a dedicated component for the Cycle Efficiency Benchmark chart using Recharts.
Integrated the CycleAchievementChart and touch swipe logic into the main modular App component.
Updated Recharts UMD script, added prop-types for browser stability, and used window.Recharts for global access.

payload.text:
Add a script tag in the head section to include the Recharts library using the source https unpkg com recharts umd Recharts js

Create a state variable named activeChartType in the main component set initially to null to track which chart popup is open

Okay, I'm thinking about adding two chart popups to the MAXXIS Production Dashboard. The `index.html` file is the entry point, using a single-file structure with React/Babel. The main component logic is in `App.tsx`, and I've got the existing `CycleAchievementChart.tsx` component to guide me.

Click listeners for "Achievement Percentage" and "Cycle Efficiency" will control the chart visibility. Finally, I'll create a `ChartModal` component with a semi-transparent overlay, centered white box, close button, and a responsive chart, dynamically displaying specific data upon click. The modular file structure seems preferred.

I'm now ensuring the `index.html` file and modular TypeScript files are consistent. Specifically, I'm updating `index.html` to reflect the updated logic in the modular files, and I'll make sure the Recharts script is at the right version. Then, I'll update `App.tsx` by importing and defining `activeChartType` and `chartData`. Finally, I'll add the necessary click handlers for the summary header and the `ChartModal` component. I'm also considering the types and data, but it seems internal state updates are all that I need.

payload.text:
Fix the following errors:

```
Uncaught TypeError: Cannot destructure property 'BarChart' of 'window.Recharts' as it is undefined.
```

**Analyzing the Recharts Error**

I'm now zeroing in on a `TypeError` stemming from Recharts. Specifically, the script is trying to destructure `BarChart` from `window.Recharts` prematurely. My focus is on determining when and how Recharts becomes available to prevent this undefined property. I'm investigating load order and potential race conditions within the initialization process.


**Investigating Dependencies Further**

I'm now diving deeper into the UMD build of Recharts and its dependencies. My current hypothesis is that the dependencies, specifically React, ReactDOM, and PropTypes, are not available on the window object when the Recharts script attempts to access them. I'm verifying the load order in `index.html` to ensure these are loaded before Recharts, and checking to see if `window.Recharts` is set correctly. The importmap is also under review.


**Resolving UMD/Importmap Conflict**

I've identified a clear conflict between the UMD script and the `importmap` for Recharts, originating from `esm.sh`. This might be due to a version mismatch. The `importmap` is pointing to a new/incorrect version, potentially conflicting with the global `window.Recharts` access that is failing. My focus now is on eliminating this conflict by either using the CDN script tags exclusively, or managing the module import. I'll remove the `importmap` and confirm correct loading. Then I'll check the `umd` script source and wait for DOM load before checking the globals.


**Refining Index.html Loading**

I'm now focusing on refining the `index.html` file to ensure Recharts loads correctly. I'm updating the script tag to use a stable version from `unpkg.com` and ensuring `PropTypes` loads before Recharts. My current focus is to defer the Recharts access until the library is fully loaded, specifically to prevent immediate execution of the destructuring. The prompt pointed out that the error arises in the browser. I suspect that the script tag in `index.html` is the primary problem.


**Confirming Global Availability**

I'm now confirming the global availability of `Recharts` when loaded via the UMD build. I'll focus on resolving the conflict between the UMD script and the modular imports within `App.tsx`. I think I'll adjust `index.html` to access `window.Recharts` only if needed, particularly since React and Babel are handled. The key is now ensuring the script executes after the UMD script is fully loaded, and checking `window.Recharts` afterward. I'm prioritizing safe access within the standalone HTML structure.


**Refining index.html Execution**

I'm now focusing on making `index.html`'s script execution more robust. I've been reviewing how to make a standalone HTML file work. Specifically, I need to prevent the immediate destructuring and ensure proper Recharts loading within the Babel script. My focus has shifted from the Vite setup to this standalone structure. I'll defer the Recharts access until the library is fully loaded.


**Simplifying Hybrid Structure**

My main focus is now the hybrid `index.html` setup. It's a mix of module and single-file approaches, which I'll simplify. I'm prioritizing the single-file structure, ensuring the correct script tags and safe Recharts access. I'll make sure `window.PropTypes` is available for the UMD bundle. I'm removing the `importmap` as it seems to be causing conflicts.


**Revising HTML & JS Structure**

I'm now revisiting `index.html` to address the immediate destructuring error. The script tries to access `window.Recharts` before it's ready. I'll either move the destructuring into the component to delay the execution or use a getter. This should eliminate the premature access error by verifying script loading. My focus is on ensuring the scripts are loaded correctly within a standalone file.


**Updating HTML/JS Structure**

I'm now focusing on a robust `index.html` and `App.tsx` structure. I'll synchronize changes to fix the Recharts issue. Specifically, I'll update the `index.html` to load `prop-types` and Recharts using CDNs and ensure safe `window.Recharts` access. In `App.tsx`, imports are standard. The fix involves using `window.Recharts`, and destructuring components within the `App` component, after a check to confirm availability. Recharts 2.x is standard, so I'll avoid 3.x.


**Refining Script Loading**

I'm now zeroing in on the script loading within `index.html`, which seems to be the root of the Recharts undefined error. The problem stems from the direct destructuring of `window.Recharts` before the library is ready. I'll need to refactor the script loading in `index.html` and ensure safe access to `window.Recharts`, perhaps with a check or by delaying the destructuring. The `importmap` is redundant. I'll make sure Recharts is available.

To fix the `Uncaught ReferenceError: Recharts is not defined` and the destructuring error, we need to ensure the Recharts library and its mandatory dependency, `prop-types`, are loaded in the correct order before the application script executes. We also need to move the destructuring logic inside the component or use a safer access pattern, as top-level destructuring in Babel scripts can sometimes trigger before the CDN-loaded libraries are fully initialized on the `window` object.

I will also ensure that the `onClick` listeners for the summary headers are implemented to trigger the requested pop-up charts.

entries.description: Fixed Recharts initialization by adding Prop-Types dependency, updating the script load order, and using a safer access pattern for the Recharts components. Added onClick handlers to summary headers for pop-up charts as requested.
